title: Func<T, TResult>
instruction:
`Func<TIn, TOut>` is a built-in delegate for a function that takes input and returns output.

Run this.
successMessage: Func<int,int> is a delegate for a function taking int and returning int. The last type parameter is always the return type.

title: Action<T>
instruction:
`Action<T>` is a delegate for a function that returns nothing (void).

Run this.
successMessage: Action has no return type. Action<string, int> takes a string and int. Use Action when you only want side effects.

title: Passing functions to methods
instruction:
Accept delegates as parameters for flexible, reusable code.

Run this.
successMessage: Filter accepts any predicate. Pass a lambda inline or a named method. This is the foundation of functional programming in C#.

title: Multicast delegates
instruction:
A delegate can hold multiple methods and invoke them all.

Run this.
successMessage: += adds a method to the delegate. When invoked, all three run. -= removes a method. This multicast capability is the foundation of events.

title: Events
instruction:
Events use delegates but restrict callers to += and -= only.

Run this.
successMessage: event restricts callers to subscribe (+=) and unsubscribe (-=) only — they cannot invoke or replace the delegate. This is the observer pattern in C#.

title: EventHandler pattern
instruction:
The standard .NET event pattern uses EventHandler<TEventArgs>.

Run this.
successMessage: EventArgs carries event data. EventHandler<T> is the standard .NET event delegate. sender is the source object.

title: Closures in lambdas
instruction:
Lambdas capture variables from their enclosing scope.

Run this.
successMessage: The lambda captures multiplier by reference. When multiplier changes to 10, the lambda sees 10. Closures capture variables, not values.

title: Predicate<T>
instruction:
`Predicate<T>` is a built-in delegate for bool-returning functions.

Run this.
successMessage: Predicate<T> is equivalent to Func<T,bool>. FindAll and Find on List use Predicate. The LINQ equivalents Where and First are more commonly used today.
